2	  "body": "(This question was first raised in the [developmental stage ontologies](https://github.com/obophenotype/developmental-stage-ontologies/pull/102#discussion_r1799308335) repo, but as a side remark on a PR. It deserves its own discussion.)\r\n\r\nPart of the steps that are needed to build Composite Metazoan is to create “bridging axioms” that link the taxon-neutral terms in Uberon to their taxon-specific counterparts in the the taxon-specific ontologies (FBbt, FBdv, WBbt, XAO, etc.).\r\n\r\nFor almost all terms, that link is of the form:\r\n\r\n> {taxon-specific term} **EquivalentTo:** {Uberon term} _and_ ('part of' _some_ {taxon ID})\r\n\r\nBut for the terms that represent life stages (specifically '[life cycle](http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/UBERON_0000104)' and '[life cycle stage](http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/UBERON_0000105)' and their descendants), the links can use two distinct relations depending on where the bridging axioms are generated.\r\n\r\nFor the bridging axioms that are generated in the [life stage ontologies](https://github.com/obophenotype/developmental-stage-ontologies/) repository, they are of the same form as above (in particular, they use the same `part of` relation).\r\n\r\nBut for the bridging axioms that are generated in Uberon (typically, those are the bridges that link to externally maintained, taxon-specific life stage ontologies like FBdv or WBls), the links are of the form\r\n\r\n> {taxon-specific term} **EquivalentTo:** {Uberon term} _and_ ('occurs in' _some_ {taxon ID})\r\n\r\nwhere `occurs in` is [BFO:0000066](http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/BFO_0000066).\r\n\r\nIt cannot be good that the relations used in those bridging axioms vary depending on the species (e.g. `occurs in` for _Drosophila melanogaster_, `part of` for _Gorilla gorilla_). Whatever relation is used, it should be the same for all species.",
3	  "comments": [
4	    {
5	      "id": "IC_kwDOAAidd86Sn3ix",
6	      "author": {
7	        "login": "gouttegd"
8	      },
9	      "authorAssociation": "MEMBER",
10	      "body": "@cmungall Two years ago you [suggested](https://github.com/obophenotype/uberon/issues/2428#issuecomment-1103321518) that all links between Uberon terms and taxon-specific terms should take the form of two axioms:\r\n\r\n> {taxon-specific term} **EquivalentTo:** {Uberon term} **and** ('in taxon' _some_ {taxon ID})\r\n> {taxon-specific term} **SubClassOf:** ({relation} _some_ {taxon ID})\r\n\r\nwhere _relation_ would be 'part of' for continuants and 'occurs in' for occurrents. Is that still what you think?",
11	      "createdAt": "2024-11-06T14:37:11Z",
12	      "includesCreatedEdit": true,
13	      "isMinimized": false,
14	      "minimizedReason": "",
15	      "reactionGroups": [],

18	    },

21	      "author": {
22	        "login": "ddooley"
23	      },
24	      "authorAssociation": "NONE",
25	      "body": "I think 'in taxon' is the recommended relation now for attaching a life cycle to an (NCBITaxon) taxon class.  'in taxon' has no restriction on its domain.  (COB is moving to have life cycle be a subclass of process BTW).",

1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	
3	# HELPER SCRIPT TO MANAGE TAXA
4	#
5	# Uberon bridging and compositing processes require a bit of information
6	# about the taxa that are involved. To make it easier to manage those
7	# informations, they are centralised in the config/taxa.yaml file.
8	#
9	# The main contents of the config/taxa.yaml file is a list of taxa,
10	# where each taxon is represented by a structure that should look like
11	# this:
12	#
13	# ---8<-----
14	# taxon_id: NCBITaxon:1234
15	# label: <human-readable suffix to append to term labels>
16	# compositing:
17	#   unfold_over: <list of properties to unfold tax-specific terms over
18	#                (defaults to BFO:0000050, BFO:0000066)>
19	#   preserve: <list of properties that should be preserved when unfolding>
20	# bridging:
21	#   - prefix: <prefix of taxon-specific terms>
22	#     namespace: <IRI namespace the prefix expands to
23	#                 (defaults to http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/prefix_)>
24	#     name: <bridge name (defaults to lowercase prefix)
25	#   [possibly other bridges for the same taxon]
26	# --->8-----
27	#
28	# Currently, this file is used for two things:
29	#
30	# (1) automatically generate the SSSOM/T-OWL rules needed to generate
31	#     the cross-species bridge files (taxa.py make-rules);
32	#
33	# (2) automatically generate the batch file used by the
34	#     uberon:merge-species command to create the composite ontologies.
35	
36	import sys
37	import yaml
38	
39	def generate_prefix_declarations(f, taxa):
40	    """Insert SSSOM/T-OWL prefix declarations for all species."""
41	    for taxon in taxa['species']:
42	        for bridge in taxon['bridging']:
43	            prefix = bridge['prefix']
44	            namespace = bridge.get('namespace', 'http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/' + prefix + '_')
45	
46	            f.write(f"prefix {prefix}: <{namespace}>\n")
47	
48	
49	def generate_directives(f, taxa):
50	    """Insert SSSOM/T-OWL taxon declarations for all species."""
51	    for taxon_id in [t['taxon_id'] for t in taxa['species']]:
52	        f.write(f"declare({taxon_id});\n")
53	
54	
55	def generate_bridging_rules(f, taxa):
56	    """Insert SSSOM/T-OWL bridging rules for all species."""
57	    for taxon in taxa['species']:
58	        taxon_id = taxon['taxon_id']
59	        def_label = taxon['label']
60	        for bridge in taxon['bridging']:
61	            prefix = bridge['prefix']
62	            name = bridge.get('name', prefix.lower())
63	            label = bridge.get('label', def_label)
64	
65	            f.write(f"""
66	[{name}-uberon] subject=={prefix}:* object==UBERON:* {{
67	    predicate==* -> annotate(%{{subject_id}}, IAO:0000589, "%{{object_label}} ({label})");
68	    predicate==semapv:crossSpeciesExactMatch -> create_axiom("%subject_id EquivalentTo: %object_id and (%TAXREL some {taxon_id})");
69	}}
70	[{name}-cl] subject=={prefix}:* object==CL:* {{
71	    predicate==* -> annotate(%{{subject_id}}, IAO:0000589, "%{{object_label}} ({label})");
72	    predicate==semapv:crossSpeciesExactMatch -> create_axiom("%subject_id EquivalentTo: %object_id and (%TAXREL some {taxon_id})");
73	}}
74	""")
75	
76	
77	def process_rule_file(filein, fileout, taxa):
78	    """Insert all SSSOM/T-OWL instructions required to generate
79	        cross-species bridges.
80	    """
81	
82	    for line in filein:
83	        if line.strip() == '%INSERT-TAX-SPECIFIC-PREFIXES':
84	            generate_prefix_declarations(fileout, taxa)
85	        elif line.strip() == '%INSERT-TAX-SPECIFIC-DIRECTIVES':
86	            generate_directives(fileout, taxa)
87	        elif line.strip() == '%INSERT-TAX-SPECIFIC-BRIDGES':
88	            generate_bridging_rules(fileout, taxa)
89	        else:
90	            fileout.write(line)
91	
92	
93	def generate_merge_table(fileout, taxa):
94	    """Generates a batch-file for uberon:merge-species."""
95	    def_link_properties = taxa.get('defaults', {}).get('compositing', {}).get('unfold_over', ['BFO:0000050', 'BFO:0000066'])
96	    def_preserved_properties = taxa.get('defaults', {}).get('compositing', {}).get('preserve', [])
97	
98	    for taxon in taxa['species']:
99	        taxon_id = taxon['taxon_id']
100	        label = taxon['label']
101	        link_properties = ','.join(taxon.get('compositing', {}).get('unfold_over', def_link_properties))
102	        preserved_properties = ','.join(taxon.get('compositing', {}).get('preserve', def_preserved_properties))
103	
104	        fileout.write(f"{taxon_id}\t{label}\t{link_properties}\t{preserved_properties}\n")
105	
106	
107	def read_taxa_file(file):
108	    with open(file, 'r') as f:
109	        return yaml.load(f, yaml.CLoader)
110	
111	
112	usage = f"Usage: {sys.argv[0]} <make-rules TAXA TEMPLATE> | <make-merge-table TAXA>"
113	if len(sys.argv) < 2:
114	    sys.exit(usage)
115	
116	cmd = sys.argv[1]
117	if cmd == 'make-rules':
118	    if len(sys.argv) != 4:
119	        sys.exit(usage)
120	    taxa = read_taxa_file(sys.argv[2])
121	    with open(sys.argv[3], 'r') as fin:
122	        process_rule_file(fin, sys.stdout, taxa)
123	elif cmd == 'make-merge-table':
124	    if len(sys.argv) != 3:
125	        sys.exit(usage)
126	    taxa = read_taxa_file(sys.argv[2])
127	    generate_merge_table(sys.stdout, taxa)
128	else:
129	    sys.exit(usage)
130

content.content:
1	# Uberon bridge files
2	
3	Uberon provides _bridge files_ for several foreign ontologies,
4	especially species-specific anatomy ontologies (ssAOs). Each bridge file
5	contains _bridging axioms_ that establish a relationship between a
6	Uberon term and a term from the foreign ontology.
7	
8	For example, the `uberon-bridge-to-zfa` bridge contains axioms such as
9	this one:
10	
11	> ZFA:0001262 EquivalentTo: UBERON:0005564 and (BFO:0000050 some NCBITaxon:7954)
12	
13	which states that ZFA’s [gonad
14	primordium](http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/ZFA_0001262) (ZFA:0001262) is
15	equivalent to a Uberon’s [gonad
16	primordium](http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/UBERON_0005564)
17	(UBERON:0005564) that is [part
18	of](http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/BFO_0000050) a
19	[Danio](http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/NCBITaxon_7954) (NCBITaxon:7954).
20	
21	Such a bridge may be used by anyone who wants to merge Uberon and ZFA to
22	obtain an integrated ontology in which the _Danio_-specific terms in ZFA
23	are properly linked to their taxon-neutral counterparts in Uberon.
24	Internally, Uberon itself is using those bridges to produce the
25	[combined multispecies ontologies](combined_multispecies.md) such as
26	`composite-metazoan`.
27	
28	In addition to the bridging axioms proper, the bridges also contain an
29	additional annotation for each bridged term: a “OBO Foundry unique
30	label” (`IAO:0000589`). That label is automatically derived from the
31	standard `rdfs:label` of the term, to which a suffix indicating the
32	taxon is appended (for example in the case of `ZFA:0001262`, the unique
33	label is “gonad primordium (zebrafish)”). This is to ensure that, upon
34	merging Uberon with taxon-specific ontologies, all the terms in the
35	merged ontology will have a distinct label.
36	
37	Note that the bridges between the [Cell
38	Ontology](https://obophenotype.github.io/cell-ontology/) (CL) and the
39	species-specific anatomy ontologies are _also_ provided by Uberon, rather by CL
40	directly. This is because the pipeline that produces those bridges (briefly
41	described below) is entirely hosted in the Uberon repository.
42	
43	The latest bridge files can be found on
44	[GitHub](https://github.com/obophenotype/uberon/tree/master/src/ontology/bridge).
45	
46	## Sources of truth
47	
48	Each bridge has one source of truth which may be:
49	
50	* cross-references in Uberon or CL;
51	* cross-references in the foreign ontology;
52	* an externally provided _mapping set_;
53	* an externally provided _custom bridge_.
54	
55	### Cross-references in Uberon or CL
56	
57	Cross-references are currently the source of truth for most bridges. For
58	such bridges, a Uberon or CL term will carry a `oboInOwl:hasDbXref`
59	annotation (aka a “OBO-style cross-reference”) whose value is the short
60	identifier of the foreign term that is, by virtue of this
61	cross-reference, being mapped to the Uberon or CL term.
62	
63	For example, Uberon contains the following annotation assertion:
64	
65	> AnnotationAssertion(oboInOwl:hasDbXref UBERON:0005564 "ZFA:0001262")
66	
67	by which Uberon’s [gonad
68	primordium](http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/UBERON_0005564) is mapped to
69	the [equivalent term in
70	ZFA](http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/ZFA_0001262).
71	
72	The annotations are present directly in the `-edit` file and may be
73	edited by curators as they wish, using e.g. Protégé.
74	
75	Note that for an annotation such as the one above to be used when
76	producing the bridge files, the prefix used in the annotation (in this
77	example `ZFA`) must be declared in an ontology-level annotation that
78	will indicate the signification of the mapping. In the case of ZFA, the
79	declaration is as follows:
80	
81	> Annotation(oboInOwl:treat-xrefs-as-reverse-genus-differentia "ZFA part\_of NCBITaxon:7954")
82	
83	and it indicates that any mapping between a Uberon term and a term from
84	ZFA should result, when producing the bridge files, in a axiom that
85	states that the ZFA term is equivalent to the intersection of the Uberon
86	term and the existential restriction `part_of some Danio`.
87	
88	Curators should not normally have to worry about declaring the prefixes
89	of foreign ontologies that Uberon provides bridges for, unless they want
90	to create a completely new bridge (which presumably should not be needed
91	often). But for reference, here are the other possible declaration
92	annotations and the meaning they give to the mappings:
93	
94	* `oboInOwl:treat-xrefs-as-equivalent "PFX"`: indicates that terms in
95	  the _PFX_ namespace are equivalent to the Uberon or CL terms they are
96	  mapped to;
97	* `oboInOwl:treat-xrefs-as-is_a "PFX"`: indicates that terms in the
98	  _PFX_ namespace are parents of the Uberon or CL terms they are mapped
99	  to;
100	* `oboInOwl:treat-xrefs-as-has-subclass "PFX"`: indicates that terms in
101	  the _PFX_ namespaces are subclasses of the Uberon or CL terms they are
102	  mapped to.
103	
104	### Cross-references in the foreign ontology
105	
106	Some foreign ontologies maintain their own mappings with Uberon and/or
107	CL, using the same cross-reference-based system as used in Uberon and CL
108	and described above. In such cases, when generating the bridges Uberon
109	obtains the cross-references from the foreign ontologies and then
110	derives the bridging axioms from them in the same way as is done for
111	cross-references maintained internally in Uberon and CL.
112	
113	Uberon or CL editors wishing to amend the mappings with an ontology that
114	maintain its own mappings should contact the editors of that ontology
115	directly, as the mappings cannot be edited directly within Uberon or CL.
116	Adding a cross-reference in Uberon or CL is possible but will have no
117	effect on the bridges.
118	
119	### Externally provided mapping sets
120	
121	Some foreign ontologies maintain their own mappings with Uberon and/or
122	CL, and provide the mappings in the form of a
123	[SSSOM](https://mapping-commons.github.io/sssom/) mapping set. For
124	such ontologies, Uberon simply uses the mapping set as provided and
125	derives the bridging axioms from it. In that case, Uberon or CL editors
126	wishing to amend the mappings should contact the editors of the foreign
127	ontology, as the mappings cannot be edited directly within Uberon/CL.
128	Adding a cross-reference in Uberon or CL will have _no_ effect.
129	
130	### Externally provided custom bridges
131	
132	Some foreign sources provide “custom” or “complex” bridge files that
133	they generate on their side using whatever pipeline they need. Uberon
134	simply uses those bridge files as they are. As for the case of
135	externally provided mapping sets, Uberon or CL editors wishing to amend
136	those bridges should contact the upstream source.
137	
138	## The bridges

190	| `cl-bridge-to-kupo.owl` | [Kidney and urinary pathway ontology](https://jbiomedsem.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/2041-1480-2-S2-S7) (KUPO) | KUPO xrefs in CL |

192	| `cl-bridge-to-ncit.owl` | [NCI thesaurus OBO edition](http://obofoundry.org/ontology/ncit) (NCIT) | NCIT xrefs in CL |

197	
198	## Producing the bridges
199	
200	This section is a (very) brief overview of the pipeline that generates
201	the bridge files.
202	
203	The pipeline may be triggered by invoking the Make target
204	`refresh-bridges` (whilst in the in `src/ontology` directory, where the
205	Makefile is located). Here is what happens then:
206	
207	1. Production of a SSSOM mapping set from the Uberon cross-references.
208	   Cross-references are extracted directly from the Uberon `-edit` file
209	   and turned into a SSSOM mapping set. This takes care of all the
210	   mappings with the foreign ontologies for which Uberon is the source
211	   of truth.
212	2. Production of a SSSOM mapping sets from ontologies that maintain
213	   their own mappings as cross-references. The foreign ontologies are
214	   downloaded and their cross-references are extracted in the same way
215	   as the Uberon cross-references are. Note that currently there is no
216	   such ontology.
217	3. Fetching of externally maintained mapping sets. For ontologies that
218	   maintain their own mappings and provide a SSSOM mapping set directly,
219	   we fetch the mapping set as it is. This currently concerns FBbt and
220	   CL (the CL set brings with it the mappings between CL and all the
221	   species-specific ontologies).
222	4. Merging of all the mapping sets. We merge the SSSOM mapping set that
223	   was derived from the Uberon cross-references (step 1), the sets that
224	   were derived from the cross-references of foreign ontologies
225	   (step 2), and the sets that we obtained directly from foreign
226	   ontologies (step 3).
227	5. Production of the bridges. We apply a large SSSOM/T-OWL ruleset
228	   (derived from `bridge/bridges.rules`, see below for more details about
229	   that ruleset) to produce bridging axioms from the mappings in the
230	   combined mapping set obtained at step 4.
231	6. Dispatching into individual bridges. The axioms produced at step 5
232	   are written to different bridges depending on the ontologies they are
233	   bridging (e.g., an axiom that bridges a Uberon term and a ZFA term
234	   will be written to the `uberon-bridge-to-zfa.owl` bridge file).
235	7. Fetching the custom bridges. Independently of all the previous steps,
236	   the “custom” bridges are fetched from their upstream source.
237	
238	Note that steps 2, 3, and 7 only happen when the Make variable `IMP` is
239	set to `true` (which is always the case when the pipeline is explicitly
240	triggered with `make refresh-bridges`). When `IMP` is set to `false`,
241	the bridge files are re-generated using only locally available resources
242	previously committed to the repository.
243	
244	Uberon maintainers should run `make refresh-bridges` periodically to
245	refresh external resources and commit refreshed versions to the
246	repository, similarly to what is needed to refresh the imports.
247	
248	### The bridging SSSOM/T-OWL ruleset
249	
250	The SSSOM/T-OWL ruleset mentioned in step 5 above is where most of the
251	logic for the production of bridge files is described. For a general
252	overview of the SSSOM/T-OWL language, please refer to the corresponding
253	[documentation](https://incenp.org/dvlpt/sssom-java/sssom-ext/sssomt-owl.html)
254	in the SSSOM-Java project.
255	
256	Because the ruleset is highly repetitive, with many almost identical
257	rules for every species we need to bridge to, it is partially
258	automatically generated from a taxa list found in the `config/taxa.yaml`
259	file.
260	
261	An entry in the taxa list should look like the following:
262	
263	```yaml
264	taxon_id: NCBITaxon:7227
265	label: D melanogaster
266	bridging:
267	  - prefix: FBbt
268	    namespace: http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FBbt_
269	    label: D melanogaster
270	    name: fbbt
271	  - prefix: FBdv
272	    namespace: http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FBdv_
273	    label: D melanogaster
274	    name: fbdv
275	```
276	
277	* `taxon_id` is the NCBI taxonomic identifier for the species we are
278	  bridging to.
279	* `label` is the human-readable name of the taxon.
280	* `bridging` is the list of bridges (there may be more than one, as in
281	  example above) for that taxon.
282	
283	Each bridge is in turn described by:
284	
285	* the `prefix` identifying the terms the bridge should include;
286	* the corresponding `namespace` the prefix expands to;
287	* the human-readable `label` of the taxon that should be appended to the
288	  label of each bridged term to form the “OBO Foundry Unique Label”;
289	* the `name` of the bridge, which will be used to form the name of the
290	  bridge file (`uberon-bridge-to-NAME.owl`).
291	
292	So, the example above states that `FBbt:*` terms (terms in the
293	`http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FBbt_` namespace) mapped to Uberon terms
294	should yield bridging axioms in a `uberon-bridge-to-fbbt.owl` bridge
295	file, while `FBdv:*` terms (in the
296	`http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FBdv_` namespace) mapped to Uberon terms
297	should yield bridging axioms in a `uberon-bridge-to-fbdv.owl` file. In
298	all cases, the bridged terms should be annotated with a “OBO Foundry
299	Unique Label” annotation of the form “Uberon label (D melanogaster)”.
300	
301	Note that:
302	
303	* If a bridge does not have an explicit `namespace`, a default namespace
304	  of `http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/PREFIX_` is used.
305	* If a bridge does not have an explicit `label`, the top-level `label`
306	  for the taxon is used.
307	* If a bridge does not have an explicit `name`, the default name is the
308	  lowercase version of the prefix.
309	
310	Those default rules mean that the example above can be written more
311	simply as:
312	
313	```yaml
314	taxon_id: NCBITaxon:7227
315	label: D melanogaster
316	bridging:
317	  - prefix: FBbt
318	  - prefix: FBdv
319	```
320

# Uberon bridge files

Uberon provides _bridge files_ for several foreign ontologies,
especially species-specific anatomy ontologies (ssAOs). Each bridge file
contains _bridging axioms_ that establish a relationship between a
Uberon term and a term from the foreign ontology.

For example, the `uberon-bridge-to-zfa` bridge contains axioms such as
this one:

> ZFA:0001262 EquivalentTo: UBERON:0005564 and (BFO:0000050 some NCBITaxon:7954)

which states that ZFA’s [gonad
primordium](http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/ZFA_0001262) (ZFA:0001262) is
equivalent to a Uberon’s [gonad
primordium](http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/UBERON_0005564)
(UBERON:0005564) that is [part
of](http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/BFO_0000050) a
[Danio](http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/NCBITaxon_7954) (NCBITaxon:7954).

Such a bridge may be used by anyone who wants to merge Uberon and ZFA to
obtain an integrated ontology in which the _Danio_-specific terms in ZFA
are properly linked to their taxon-neutral counterparts in Uberon.
Internally, Uberon itself is using those bridges to produce the
[combined multispecies ontologies](combined_multispecies.md) such as
`composite-metazoan`.

In addition to the bridging axioms proper, the bridges also contain an
additional annotation for each bridged term: a “OBO Foundry unique
label” (`IAO:0000589`). That label is automatically derived from the
standard `rdfs:label` of the term, to which a suffix indicating the
taxon is appended (for example in the case of `ZFA:0001262`, the unique
label is “gonad primordium (zebrafish)”). This is to ensure that, upon
merging Uberon with taxon-specific ontologies, all the terms in the
merged ontology will have a distinct label.

Note that the bridges between the [Cell
Ontology](https://obophenotype.github.io/cell-ontology/) (CL) and the
species-specific anatomy ontologies are _also_ provided by Uberon, rather by CL
directly. This is because the pipeline that produces those bridges (briefly
described below) is entirely hosted in the Uberon repository.

The latest bridge files can be found on
[GitHub](https://github.com/obophenotype/uberon/tree/master/src/ontology/bridge).

## Sources of truth

Each bridge has one source of truth which may be:

* cross-references in Uberon or CL;
* cross-references in the foreign ontology;
* an externally provided _mapping set_;
* an externally provided _custom bridge_.

### Cross-references in Uberon or CL

Cross-references are currently the source of truth for most bridges. For
such bridges, a Uberon or CL term will carry a `oboInOwl:hasDbXref`
annotation (aka a “OBO-style cross-reference”) whose value is the short
identifier of the foreign term that is, by virtue of this
cross-reference, being mapped to the Uberon or CL term.

For example, Uberon contains the following annotation assertion:

> AnnotationAssertion(oboInOwl:hasDbXref UBERON:0005564 "ZFA:0001262")

by which Uberon’s [gonad
primordium](http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/UBERON_0005564) is mapped to
the [equivalent term in
ZFA](http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/ZFA_0001262).

The annotations are present directly in the `-edit` file and may be
edited by curators as they wish, using e.g. Protégé.

Note that for an annotation such as the one above to be used when
producing the bridge files, the prefix used in the annotation (in this
example `ZFA`) must be declared in an ontology-level annotation that
will indicate the signification of the mapping. In the case of ZFA, the
declaration is as follows:

> Annotation(oboInOwl:treat-xrefs-as-reverse-genus-differentia "ZFA part\_of NCBITaxon:7954")

and it indicates that any mapping between a Uberon term and a term from
ZFA should result, when producing the bridge files, in a axiom that
states that the ZFA term is equivalent to the intersection of the Uberon
term and the existential restriction `part_of some Danio`.

Curators should not normally have to worry about declaring the prefixes
of foreign ontologies that Uberon provides bridges for, unless they want
to create a completely new bridge (which presumably should not be needed
often). But for reference, here are the other possible declaration
annotations and the meaning they give to the mappings:

* `oboInOwl:treat-xrefs-as-equivalent "PFX"`: indicates that terms in
  the _PFX_ namespace are equivalent to the Uberon or CL terms they are
  mapped to;
* `oboInOwl:treat-xrefs-as-is_a "PFX"`: indicates that terms in the
  _PFX_ namespace are parents of the Uberon or CL terms they are mapped
  to;
* `oboInOwl:treat-xrefs-as-has-subclass "PFX"`: indicates that terms in
  the _PFX_ namespaces are subclasses of the Uberon or CL terms they are
  mapped to.

### Cross-references in the foreign ontology

Some foreign ontologies maintain their own mappings with Uberon and/or
CL, using the same cross-reference-based system as used in Uberon and CL
and described above. In such cases, when generating the bridges Uberon
obtains the cross-references from the foreign ontologies and then
derives the bridging axioms from them in the same way as is done for
cross-references maintained internally in Uberon and CL.

Uberon or CL editors wishing to amend the mappings with an ontology that
maintain its own mappings should contact the editors of that ontology
directly, as the mappings cannot be edited directly within Uberon or CL.
Adding a cross-reference in Uberon or CL is possible but will have no
effect on the bridges.

### Externally provided mapping sets

Some foreign ontologies maintain their own mappings with Uberon and/or
CL, and provide the mappings in the form of a
[SSSOM](https://mapping-commons.github.io/sssom/) mapping set. For
such ontologies, Uberon simply uses the mapping set as provided and
derives the bridging axioms from it. In that case, Uberon or CL editors
wishing to amend the mappings should contact the editors of the foreign
ontology, as the mappings cannot be edited directly within Uberon/CL.
Adding a cross-reference in Uberon or CL will have _no_ effect.

### Externally provided custom bridges

Some foreign sources provide “custom” or “complex” bridge files that
they generate on their side using whatever pipeline they need. Uberon
simply uses those bridge files as they are. As for the case of
externally provided mapping sets, Uberon or CL editors wishing to amend
those bridges should contact the upstream source.

## The bridges

The following is a list of all the current bridges, along with their
current source of truth.

### Bridges with Uberon

| Bridge name | Foreign ontology | Source of truth |
| ----------- | ---------------- | --------------- |
| `uberon-bridge-to-aeo.owl` | [Anatomical entity ontology](http://obofoundry.org/ontology/aeo) (AEO) | AEO xrefs in Uberon |
| `uberon-bridge-to-bfo.owl` | [Basic formal ontology](http://obofoundry.org/ontology/bfo) (BFO) | BFO xrefs in Uberon |
| `uberon-bridge-to-caro.owl` | [Common anatomy reference ontology](http://obofoundry.org/ontology/caro) (CARO) | CARO xrefs in Uberon |
| `uberon-bridge-to-dhba.owl` | [Developing human brain atlas](https://www.brainspan.org/) (DHBA) | DHBA xrefs in Uberon |
| `uberon-bridge-to-dmba.owl` | [Developing mouse brain atlas](https://developingmouse.brain-map.org/) (DMBA) | Externally provided custom bridge, maintained [here](https://github.com/brain-bican/developing_mouse_brain_atlas_ontology) |
| `uberon-bridge-to-ehdaa2.owl` | [Human developmental anatomy](http://obofoundry.org/ontology/ehdaa2.html) (EHDAA2) | EHDAA2 xrefs in Uberon |
| `uberon-bridge-to-emapa.owl` | [Mouse developmental anatomy ontology](http://obofoundry.org/ontology/emapa) (EMAPA) | EMAPA xrefs in Uberon |
| `uberon-bridge-to-fbbt.owl` | [Drosophila anatomy ontology](http://obofoundry.org/ontology/fbbt) (FBbt) | [FBbt-maintained](https://github.com/FlyBase/drosophila-anatomy-developmental-ontology) mapping set |
| `uberon-bridge-to-fbdv.owl` | [Drosophila development ontology](http://obofoundry.org/ontology/fbdv) (FBdv) | FBdv xrefs in Uberon |
| `uberon-bridge-to-fma.owl` | [Foundational model of anatomy ontology](http://obofoundry.org/ontology/fma) (FMA) | FMA xrefs in Uberon |
| `uberon-bridge-to-go.owl` | [Gene ontology](http://obofoundry.org/ontology/go) (GO) | GO xrefs in Uberon |
| `uberon-bridge-to-hao.owl` | [Hymenoptera anatomy ontology](http://obofoundry.org/ontology/hao) (HAO) | HAO xrefs in Uberon |
| `uberon-bridge-to-hba.owl` | [Human brain atlas](https://human.brain-map.org/) (HBA) | HBA xrefs in Uberon |
| `uberon-bridge-to-hsapdv.owl` | [Human developmental stages](http://obofoundry.org/ontology/hsapdv) (HsapDv) | [SSLSO-maintained](https://github.com/obophenotype/developmental-stage-ontologies) mapping set |

## Producing the bridges

This section is a (very) brief overview of the pipeline that generates
the bridge files.

The pipeline may be triggered by invoking the Make target
`refresh-bridges` (whilst in the in `src/ontology` directory, where the
Makefile is located). Here is what happens then:

1. Production of a SSSOM mapping set from the Uberon cross-references.
   Cross-references are extracted directly from the Uberon `-edit` file
   and turned into a SSSOM mapping set. This takes care of all the
   mappings with the foreign ontologies for which Uberon is the source
   of truth.
2. Production of a SSSOM mapping sets from ontologies that maintain
   their own mappings as cross-references. The foreign ontologies are
   downloaded and their cross-references are extracted in the same way
   as the Uberon cross-references are. Note that currently there is no
   such ontology.
3. Fetching of externally maintained mapping sets. For ontologies that
   maintain their own mappings and provide a SSSOM mapping set directly,
   we fetch the mapping set as it is. This currently concerns FBbt and
   CL (the CL set brings with it the mappings between CL and all the
   species-specific ontologies).
4. Merging of all the mapping sets. We merge the SSSOM mapping set that
   was derived from the Uberon cross-references (step 1), the sets that
   were derived from the cross-references of foreign ontologies
   (step 2), and the sets that we obtained directly from foreign
   ontologies (step 3).
5. Production of the bridges. We apply a large SSSOM/T-OWL ruleset
   (derived from `bridge/bridges.rules`, see below for more details about
   that ruleset) to produce bridging axioms from the mappings in the
   combined mapping set obtained at step 4.
6. Dispatching into individual bridges. The axioms produced at step 5
   are written to different bridges depending on the ontologies they are
   bridging (e.g., an axiom that bridges a Uberon term and a ZFA term
   will be written to the `uberon-bridge-to-zfa.owl` bridge file).
7. Fetching the custom bridges. Independently of all the previous steps,
   the “custom” bridges are fetched from their upstream source.

Note that steps 2, 3, and 7 only happen when the Make variable `IMP` is
set to `true` (which is always the case when the pipeline is explicitly
triggered with `make refresh-bridges`). When `IMP` is set to `false`,
the bridge files are re-generated using only locally available resources
previously committed to the repository.

Uberon maintainers should run `make refresh-bridges` periodically to
refresh external resources and commit refreshed versions to the
repository, similarly to what is needed to refresh the imports.

### The bridging SSSOM/T-OWL ruleset

The SSSOM/T-OWL ruleset mentioned in step 5 above is where most of the
logic for the production of bridge files is described. For a general
overview of the SSSOM/T-OWL language, please refer to the corresponding
[documentation](https://incenp.org/dvlpt/sssom-java/sssom-ext/sssomt-owl.html)
in the SSSOM-Java project.

Because the ruleset is highly repetitive, with many almost identical
rules for every species we need to bridge to, it is partially
automatically generated from a taxa list found in the `config/taxa.yaml`
file.

An entry in the taxa list should look like the following:

```yaml
taxon_id: NCBITaxon:7227
label: D melanogaster
bridging:
  - prefix: FBbt
    namespace: http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FBbt_
    label: D melanogaster
    name: fbbt
  - prefix: FBdv
    namespace: http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FBdv_
    label: D melanogaster
    name: fbdv
```

* `taxon_id` is the NCBI taxonomic identifier for the species we are
  bridging to.
* `label` is the human-readable name of the taxon.
* `bridging` is the list of bridges (there may be more than one, as in
  example above) for that taxon.

Each bridge is in turn described by:

* the `prefix` identifying the terms the bridge should include;
* the corresponding `namespace` the prefix expands to;
* the human-readable `label` of the taxon that should be appended to the
  label of each bridged term to form the “OBO Foundry Unique Label”;
* the `name` of the bridge, which will be used to form the name of the
  bridge file (`uberon-bridge-to-NAME.owl`).

So, the example above states that `FBbt:*` terms (terms in the
`http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FBbt_` namespace) mapped to Uberon terms
should yield bridging axioms in a `uberon-bridge-to-fbbt.owl` bridge
file, while `FBdv:*` terms (in the
`http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FBdv_` namespace) mapped to Uberon terms
should yield bridging axioms in a `uberon-bridge-to-fbdv.owl` file. In
all cases, the bridged terms should be annotated with a “OBO Foundry
Unique Label” annotation of the form “Uberon label (D melanogaster)”.

Note that:

* If a bridge does not have an explicit `namespace`, a default namespace
  of `http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/PREFIX_` is used.
* If a bridge does not have an explicit `label`, the top-level `label`
  for the taxon is used.
* If a bridge does not have an explicit `name`, the default name is the
  lowercase version of the prefix.

Those default rules mean that the example above can be written more
simply as:

```yaml
taxon_id: NCBITaxon:7227
label: D melanogaster
bridging:
  - prefix: FBbt
  - prefix: FBdv
```

tool_use_result.originalFile:
# Uberon bridge files

Uberon provides _bridge files_ for several foreign ontologies,
especially species-specific anatomy ontologies (ssAOs). Each bridge file
contains _bridging axioms_ that establish a relationship between a
Uberon term and a term from the foreign ontology.

For example, the `uberon-bridge-to-zfa` bridge contains axioms such as
this one:

> ZFA:0001262 EquivalentTo: UBERON:0005564 and (BFO:0000050 some NCBITaxon:7954)

which states that ZFA’s [gonad
primordium](http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/ZFA_0001262) (ZFA:0001262) is
equivalent to a Uberon’s [gonad
primordium](http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/UBERON_0005564)
(UBERON:0005564) that is [part
of](http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/BFO_0000050) a
[Danio](http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/NCBITaxon_7954) (NCBITaxon:7954).

Such a bridge may be used by anyone who wants to merge Uberon and ZFA to
obtain an integrated ontology in which the _Danio_-specific terms in ZFA
are properly linked to their taxon-neutral counterparts in Uberon.
Internally, Uberon itself is using those bridges to produce the
[combined multispecies ontologies](combined_multispecies.md) such as
`composite-metazoan`.

In addition to the bridging axioms proper, the bridges also contain an
additional annotation for each bridged term: a “OBO Foundry unique
label” (`IAO:0000589`). That label is automatically derived from the
standard `rdfs:label` of the term, to which a suffix indicating the
taxon is appended (for example in the case of `ZFA:0001262`, the unique
label is “gonad primordium (zebrafish)”). This is to ensure that, upon
merging Uberon with taxon-specific ontologies, all the terms in the
merged ontology will have a distinct label.

Note that the bridges between the [Cell
Ontology](https://obophenotype.github.io/cell-ontology/) (CL) and the
species-specific anatomy ontologies are _also_ provided by Uberon, rather by CL
directly. This is because the pipeline that produces those bridges (briefly
described below) is entirely hosted in the Uberon repository.

The latest bridge files can be found on
[GitHub](https://github.com/obophenotype/uberon/tree/master/src/ontology/bridge).

## Sources of truth

Each bridge has one source of truth which may be:

* cross-references in Uberon or CL;
* cross-references in the foreign ontology;
* an externally provided _mapping set_;
* an externally provided _custom bridge_.

### Cross-references in Uberon or CL

Cross-references are currently the source of truth for most bridges. For
such bridges, a Uberon or CL term will carry a `oboInOwl:hasDbXref`
annotation (aka a “OBO-style cross-reference”) whose value is the short
identifier of the foreign term that is, by virtue of this
cross-reference, being mapped to the Uberon or CL term.

For example, Uberon contains the following annotation assertion:

> AnnotationAssertion(oboInOwl:hasDbXref UBERON:0005564 "ZFA:0001262")

by which Uberon’s [gonad
primordium](http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/UBERON_0005564) is mapped to
the [equivalent term in
ZFA](http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/ZFA_0001262).

The annotations are present directly in the `-edit` file and may be
edited by curators as they wish, using e.g. Protégé.

Note that for an annotation such as the one above to be used when
producing the bridge files, the prefix used in the annotation (in this
example `ZFA`) must be declared in an ontology-level annotation that
will indicate the signification of the mapping. In the case of ZFA, the
declaration is as follows:

> Annotation(oboInOwl:treat-xrefs-as-reverse-genus-differentia "ZFA part\_of NCBITaxon:7954")

and it indicates that any mapping between a Uberon term and a term from
ZFA should result, when producing the bridge files, in a axiom that
states that the ZFA term is equivalent to the intersection of the Uberon
term and the existential restriction `part_of some Danio`.

Curators should not normally have to worry about declaring the prefixes
of foreign ontologies that Uberon provides bridges for, unless they want
to create a completely new bridge (which presumably should not be needed
often). But for reference, here are the other possible declaration
annotations and the meaning they give to the mappings:

* `oboInOwl:treat-xrefs-as-equivalent "PFX"`: indicates that terms in
  the _PFX_ namespace are equivalent to the Uberon or CL terms they are
  mapped to;
* `oboInOwl:treat-xrefs-as-is_a "PFX"`: indicates that terms in the
  _PFX_ namespace are parents of the Uberon or CL terms they are mapped
  to;
* `oboInOwl:treat-xrefs-as-has-subclass "PFX"`: indicates that terms in
  the _PFX_ namespaces are subclasses of the Uberon or CL terms they are
  mapped to.

### Cross-references in the foreign ontology

Some foreign ontologies maintain their own mappings with Uberon and/or
CL, using the same cross-reference-based system as used in Uberon and CL
and described above. In such cases, when generating the bridges Uberon
obtains the cross-references from the foreign ontologies and then
derives the bridging axioms from them in the same way as is done for
cross-references maintained internally in Uberon and CL.

Uberon or CL editors wishing to amend the mappings with an ontology that
maintain its own mappings should contact the editors of that ontology
directly, as the mappings cannot be edited directly within Uberon or CL.
Adding a cross-reference in Uberon or CL is possible but will have no
effect on the bridges.

### Externally provided mapping sets

Some foreign ontologies maintain their own mappings with Uberon and/or
CL, and provide the mappings in the form of a
[SSSOM](https://mapping-commons.github.io/sssom/) mapping set. For
such ontologies, Uberon simply uses the mapping set as provided and
derives the bridging axioms from it. In that case, Uberon or CL editors
wishing to amend the mappings should contact the editors of the foreign
ontology, as the mappings cannot be edited directly within Uberon/CL.
Adding a cross-reference in Uberon or CL will have _no_ effect.

### Externally provided custom bridges

Some foreign sources provide “custom” or “complex” bridge files that
they generate on their side using whatever pipeline they need. Uberon
simply uses those bridge files as they are. As for the case of
externally provided mapping sets, Uberon or CL editors wishing to amend
those bridges should contact the upstream source.

## Producing the bridges

This section is a (very) brief overview of the pipeline that generates
the bridge files.

The pipeline may be triggered by invoking the Make target
`refresh-bridges` (whilst in the in `src/ontology` directory, where the
Makefile is located). Here is what happens then:

1. Production of a SSSOM mapping set from the Uberon cross-references.
   Cross-references are extracted directly from the Uberon `-edit` file
   and turned into a SSSOM mapping set. This takes care of all the
   mappings with the foreign ontologies for which Uberon is the source
   of truth.
2. Production of a SSSOM mapping sets from ontologies that maintain
   their own mappings as cross-references. The foreign ontologies are
   downloaded and their cross-references are extracted in the same way
   as the Uberon cross-references are. Note that currently there is no
   such ontology.
3. Fetching of externally maintained mapping sets. For ontologies that
   maintain their own mappings and provide a SSSOM mapping set directly,
   we fetch the mapping set as it is. This currently concerns FBbt and
   CL (the CL set brings with it the mappings between CL and all the
   species-specific ontologies).
4. Merging of all the mapping sets. We merge the SSSOM mapping set that
   was derived from the Uberon cross-references (step 1), the sets that
   were derived from the cross-references of foreign ontologies
   (step 2), and the sets that we obtained directly from foreign
   ontologies (step 3).
5. Production of the bridges. We apply a large SSSOM/T-OWL ruleset
   (derived from `bridge/bridges.rules`, see below for more details about
   that ruleset) to produce bridging axioms from the mappings in the
   combined mapping set obtained at step 4.
6. Dispatching into individual bridges. The axioms produced at step 5
   are written to different bridges depending on the ontologies they are
   bridging (e.g., an axiom that bridges a Uberon term and a ZFA term
   will be written to the `uberon-bridge-to-zfa.owl` bridge file).
7. Fetching the custom bridges. Independently of all the previous steps,
   the “custom” bridges are fetched from their upstream source.

Note that steps 2, 3, and 7 only happen when the Make variable `IMP` is
set to `true` (which is always the case when the pipeline is explicitly
triggered with `make refresh-bridges`). When `IMP` is set to `false`,
the bridge files are re-generated using only locally available resources
previously committed to the repository.

Uberon maintainers should run `make refresh-bridges` periodically to
refresh external resources and commit refreshed versions to the
repository, similarly to what is needed to refresh the imports.

### The bridging SSSOM/T-OWL ruleset

The SSSOM/T-OWL ruleset mentioned in step 5 above is where most of the
logic for the production of bridge files is described. For a general
overview of the SSSOM/T-OWL language, please refer to the corresponding
[documentation](https://incenp.org/dvlpt/sssom-java/sssom-ext/sssomt-owl.html)
in the SSSOM-Java project.

Because the ruleset is highly repetitive, with many almost identical
rules for every species we need to bridge to, it is partially
automatically generated from a taxa list found in the `config/taxa.yaml`
file.

An entry in the taxa list should look like the following:

```yaml
taxon_id: NCBITaxon:7227
label: D melanogaster
bridging:
  - prefix: FBbt
    namespace: http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FBbt_
    label: D melanogaster
    name: fbbt
  - prefix: FBdv
    namespace: http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FBdv_
    label: D melanogaster
    name: fbdv
```

* `taxon_id` is the NCBI taxonomic identifier for the species we are
  bridging to.
* `label` is the human-readable name of the taxon.
* `bridging` is the list of bridges (there may be more than one, as in
  example above) for that taxon.

Each bridge is in turn described by:

* the `prefix` identifying the terms the bridge should include;
* the corresponding `namespace` the prefix expands to;
* the human-readable `label` of the taxon that should be appended to the
  label of each bridged term to form the “OBO Foundry Unique Label”;
* the `name` of the bridge, which will be used to form the name of the
  bridge file (`uberon-bridge-to-NAME.owl`).

So, the example above states that `FBbt:*` terms (terms in the
`http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FBbt_` namespace) mapped to Uberon terms
should yield bridging axioms in a `uberon-bridge-to-fbbt.owl` bridge
file, while `FBdv:*` terms (in the
`http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FBdv_` namespace) mapped to Uberon terms
should yield bridging axioms in a `uberon-bridge-to-fbdv.owl` file. In
all cases, the bridged terms should be annotated with a “OBO Foundry
Unique Label” annotation of the form “Uberon label (D melanogaster)”.

Note that:

* If a bridge does not have an explicit `namespace`, a default namespace
  of `http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/PREFIX_` is used.
* If a bridge does not have an explicit `label`, the top-level `label`
  for the taxon is used.
* If a bridge does not have an explicit `name`, the default name is the
  lowercase version of the prefix.

Those default rules mean that the example above can be written more
simply as:

```yaml
taxon_id: NCBITaxon:7227
label: D melanogaster
bridging:
  - prefix: FBbt
  - prefix: FBdv
```

# Combined Multispecies Ontologies

This document describes the multi-species products of Uberon and how
they are built. It is an updated version of the documentation from the
old Uberon Wiki, especially the following pages: [Multi-species importer
ontologies](https://github.com/obophenotype/uberon/wiki/Multi-species-importer-ontologies)
and [Multi-species composite
ontologies](https://github.com/obophenotype/uberon/wiki/Multi-species-composite-ontologies).

## Types of multi-species ontologies.

Uberon provides two distinct types of multi-species products: _collected
ontologies_ and _composite ontologies_.

### Collected ontologies

A _collected ontology_ is obtained by merging Uberon itself, the [Cell
Ontology](https://obophenotype.github.io/cell-ontology/) (CL), one or
several taxon-specific anatomy ontologies, along with, for each
concerned taxon, the corresponding ontology of developmental stages (if
such an ontology exists) and the corresponding [bridge
files](bridges.md).

For example, the `collected-drosophila.owl` ontology is the result of
merging:

* Uberon,
* CL,
* the [Drosophila Anatomy Ontology](https://github.com/FlyBase/drosophila-anatomy-developmental-ontology) (FBbt),
* the [bridge between Uberon and FBbt](https://github.com/obophenotype/uberon/blob/master/src/ontology/bridge/uberon-bridge-to-fbbt.owl),
* the [bridge between CL and FBbt](https://github.com/obophenotype/uberon/blob/master/src/ontology/bridge/cl-bridge-to-fbbt.owl),
* the [Drosophila Developmental Ontology](https://github.com/FlyBase/drosophila-anatomy-developmental-ontology) (FBdv),
* and the [bridge between Uberon and FBdv](https://github.com/obophenotype/uberon/blob/master/src/ontology/bridge/uberon-bridge-to-fbdv.owl).

> Note: Collected ontologies used to be called “importer ontologies”,
> because they were made by using OWL `Import` statements to gather the
> different component ontologies. This no longer reflects how those
> ontologies are built (see further below for some details about the
> pipeline that builds them), so “collected ontologies” is now
> preferred.

#### Available collected ontologies

Uberon defines several collected ontologies for different taxonomic
levels. The custom [Uberon
Makefile](https://github.com/obophenotype/uberon/blob/master/src/ontology/uberon.Makefile),
in its “Composite pipeline” section, is the definitive source of truth
for the various collected ontologies that are available, but as of
January 2025 the list is as follows (for simplicity, bridge files are not
mentioned):

| Product | Components |
| ------- | ---------- |
| collected-drosophila | FBbt + FBdv |
| collected-worm | WBbt + WBls |
| collected-zebrafish | ZFA |
| collected-xenopus | XAO |
| collected-human | EHDAA2 + AEO |
| collected-mouse | EMAPA + MmusDv |
| collected-adult-mammal | MA |
| collected-embryonic-mammal | collected-human + collected-mouse |
| collected-mammal | collected-adult-mammal + collected-embryonic-mammal + DHBA + DMBA + HBA + MBA + PBA |
| collected-anamniote | collected-zebrafish + collected-xenopus |
| collected-amniote | collected-mammal |
| collected-tetrapod | collected-amniote + collected-xenopus |
| collected-vertebrate | collected-tetrapod + collected-zebrafish |
| collected-metazoan | collected-vertebrate + collected-drosophila + collected-worm + CEPH + CTENO + PORO |
| collected-lifestages | Uberon’s life-stages subset + all available species-specific life stages ontologies |

Note that only `collected-metazoan`, `collected-vertebrate`, and
`collected-lifestages` are regularly built and provided as release
artifacts, available through permanent URLs in OBO, OWL, and
Obograph-JSON formats. Other products, if they are needed, must be built
on demand (see further below for instructions on how to do that).

#### Advantages

Collected ontologies

* retain all logical and non-logical axioms of the source ontologies;
  reasoning “just works”;
* allow incorporation at desired level of granularity.

#### Disadvantages

* Collected ontologies are highly latticed and difficult to navigate.
* Differences between a species classand the Uberon parent is often
  trivial or non-existent, resulting in duplicated portions of the
  hierarchy.
* Many tools are not configured to show the “OBO Foundry unique label”
  provided by the bridge files, which means that for example
  `collected-metazoan.owl` will display 10 or so classes with a
  `rdfs:label` of “brain”.

### Composite ontologies

_Composite ontologies_ were designed to address the redundancy problems
inherent with collected ontologies.

#### Principle

A composite ontology is derived from the corresponding collected
ontology (for example, `composite-drosophila` is derived from
`collected-drosophila`). The key part of the process by which a
composite ontology is derived is to replace, whenever possible, the
taxon-specific terms coming from the taxon-specific ontologies by
anonymous class expressions that use the corresponding taxon-neutral
term from Uberon.

For example, let us consider the FBbt term “ovary” (`FBbt:00004865`): it
is mapped to the Uberon term “ovary” (`UBERON:0000992`), which means
that `collected-drosophila` contains the following axiom (provided by the
bridge between Uberon and FBbt):

```
FBbt:00004865 EquivalentTo: UBERON:0000992 and (part_of some NCBITaxon:7227)
```

(`NCBITaxon:7227` being the identifier for the _Drosophila melanogaster_
taxon).

To produce `composite-drosophila`, we remove the `FBbt:00004865` class,
and rewrites all axioms that refer to it to make them use the anonymous
expression the class is equivalent to instead.  So the following axiom,
which states that the “oviduct” (`FBbt:00004911`) is continuous with the
fly ovary:

```
FBbt:00004911 SubClassOf: continuous_with some FBbt:00004865
```

gets rewritten as

```
FBbt:00004911 SubClassOf: continous_with some (UBERON:00009992 and (part_of some NCBITaxon:7227))
```

The figure below illustrates the resulting differences between a
collected ontology and a composite ontology. On the left, the
collected-drosophila ontology contains, below the taxon-neutral “ovary”
(`UBERON:0000992`) term, the fly-specific “ovary” term
(`FBbt:00004865`), to which all terms related to the _Drosophila_ ovary
are attached. On the right, the composite-drosophila no longer contains
the redundant fly-specific “ovary”, and all the fly terms are directly
rattached to the taxon-neutral “ovary”.

![Collected vs Composite Hierarchies](images/collected-vs-composite.jpg)

#### Available composite ontologies

Because composite ontologies are derived from the collected ontologies,
each collected ontology has a corresponding composite ontology.
Therefore, you may refer to the list of collected ontologies above.

As for the collected ontologies, only `composite-metazoan`,
`composite-vertebrate`, and `composite-lifestages` are built regularly
and provided as pre-built artifacts. Other products, if they are needed,
must be built on demand.

#### Advantages and disadvantages

Compared to collected ontologies, composite ontologies have less
redundancy and are easier to navigate.

However, the absence of some taxon-specific terms (when a taxon-neutral
equivalent is available) may make them unsuitable for data annotation,
unless the taxon information is recorded separately. For example, if you
need to annotate a fly ovary sample, a composite ontology will _not_
contain a term that specifically represents a fly ovary – it will only
contain a term that represents a taxon-neutral ovary, and you will need
another way to record the fact that the sample is coming from a fruit
fly.

## Building the multi-species ontologies

Collected ontologies are simply a merge (performed using ROBOT’s `merge`
command) of the source ontologies.

The only particularity is that the taxon-specific ontologies are _not_
fetched directly from their canonical online locations. Instead, they
are mirrored locally, the mirrors are committed to the repository, and
all subsequent operations are done using the local mirrors. To refresh
the local mirrors, run the following command from the `src/ontology`
directory:

```sh
sh run.sh make MIR=true IMP=true all_local_imports
```

(Alternatively you may also run `sh run.sh make
refresh-external-resources` instead, which will also the mappings and
the bridge files, as well as the normal ODK import modules.)

To build a given collected ontology, simply run:

```sh
sh run.sh make tmp/collected-<name>.owl
```

A composite ontology is built by first building the corresponding
collected ontology, then

* removing all disjointness axioms;
* removing redundant taxon-specific terms as explained above (this is
  done using a [custom ROBOT plugin](https://github.com/gouttegd/uberon-robot-plugin));
* reasoning, relaxing, and reducing.

To build a given composite ontology, simply run:

```sh
sh run.sh make composite-<name>.owl
```

## Adding a species to a collected/composite ontology

(This is _not_ an exhaustive documentation, but intended as a rough
guide for future reference.)

1. Add a “local mirror” for the species-specific ontology to be
   included. Follow the examples of the Makefile rules for the existing
   local mirrors.
2. Ensure that mappings between Uberon/CL terms and the species-specific
   terms are available -- either maintained in Uberon directly, or
   fetched from a remote source (likely the species-specific ontology).
3. Add a [bridge file](bridges.md) (or _several bridges_, if needed) for
   the species-specific ontology.
4. Add the local mirror and any corresponding bridge file to the list of
   source files in the `COLLECTED_xxx_SOURCES` variable (where `xxx` is
   the name of the collected/composite ontology, e.g. `metazoan`).

input.content:
# Use `in taxon` (RO:0002162) for cross-species bridge equivalence axioms

Closes #3409.

## Background

The cross-species bridges generated by Uberon link taxon-specific terms (e.g., FBbt, ZFA, WBls) to their taxon-neutral counterparts. Up to now, the relation used in the equivalence axiom depended on the kind of term being bridged:

- For continuants, `part of` (BFO:0000050) was used.
- For life cycle (UBERON:0000104) and life cycle stage (UBERON:0000105) descendants, `occurs in` (BFO:0000066) was used.

As @gouttegd pointed out in #3409, this meant that whether a life-stage bridge used `part of` or `occurs in` depended on _where_ the bridge was generated (Uberon or developmental-stage-ontologies), not on what kind of term was being bridged. The same life stage could end up with two different bridging relations across species.

The discussion in #3409 settled on using `in taxon` (RO:0002162) consistently. The pattern adopted here is the one originally suggested by @cmungall in #2428:

> {taxon-specific term} **EquivalentTo:** {Uberon term} **and** ('in taxon' some {taxon ID})
> {taxon-specific term} **SubClassOf:** ({relation} some {taxon ID})

— `in taxon` carries the cross-species equivalence; the more specific `part of` / `occurs in` SubClassOf is retained so the taxon-relation information is not lost for downstream consumers.

## Changes

- **`src/scripts/taxa.py`** — The auto-generated SSSOM/T-OWL rules now emit two axioms per bridge instead of one:
  - `EquivalentTo: {Uberon term} and (RO:0002162 some {taxon_id})` — consistent across continuants and life stages.
  - `SubClassOf: {Uberon term} %TAXREL some {taxon_id}` — uses the existing `TAXREL` variable (still defined in `bridges.rules` as `part_of` by default, `occurs_in` for life-stage descendants).
- **`src/ontology/config/taxa.yaml`** — `defaults.compositing.unfold_over` updated from `[BFO:0000050, BFO:0000066]` to `[RO:0002162]` so the compositing pipeline (`uberon:merge-species`) unfolds taxon-specific terms over the same relation now used in the bridging axioms.
- **`src/ontology/imports/ro_terms.txt`** — Add `RO:0002012` (occurrent part of) to the imported RO terms. It is referenced by the FBdv-related imports (`src/ontology/imports/local-fbdv.owl` declares `FBdv:00018001` as a sub-property of `RO:0002012`) and is the natural counterpart to `part_of` for the life-stage / occurrent side of the SubClassOf axiom.
- **`docs/bridges.md`, `docs/combined_multispecies.md`** — Update the worked examples (ZFA gonad primordium, FBbt ovary) to reflect the new `in taxon`-based equivalence.

## Rationale checklist

- [x] Read issue #3409 and the linked issue #2428 for prior context.
- [x] Confirmed `bridges.rules` keeps the existing `TAXREL` switch (BFO:0000050 by default, BFO:0000066 for `UBERON:0000104`/`UBERON:0000105` descendants) so the new SubClassOf axiom continues to use the appropriate process-vs-continuant relation.
- [x] Verified prefix `RO: <http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/RO_>` is already declared in `bridges.rules`, so `RO:0002162` resolves correctly inside the generated SSSOM/T-OWL rules.
- [x] Checked that the existing `dosdp-patterns/dev/taxon_specific.yaml` already uses `RO:0002162`, so the new bridging relation is consistent with existing design patterns.
- [x] Confirmed `RO:0002162` carries property chains over `BFO:0000050`, `BFO:0000051`, etc., so the broader part-of / has-part hierarchy still propagates taxon membership as expected.
- [x] Updated the documentation examples in `docs/bridges.md` and `docs/combined_multispecies.md` so they no longer show stale `part_of` axioms.

## Notes for reviewers

- The SubClassOf axiom uses the existing `TAXREL` variable from `bridges.rules`; this preserves the previous (continuant ↔ part_of, life stage ↔ occurs_in) distinction, but now as a redundant (SubClass) assertion rather than a load-bearing (Equivalent) one. If reviewers prefer to drop the SubClassOf axiom entirely (i.e. only emit the `in taxon` equivalence), removing the second `create_axiom(...)` call in `taxa.py` is a one-line change.
- The `RO:0002012` import is justified by its existing use in `local-fbdv.owl` for the FBdv `substage of` relation; reviewers may want to confirm whether the new bridges produce any axiom that directly references it.

Signed off by @dragon-ai-agent.
